segment-text: Based on the book Me and Earl and the Dying Girl (Harry Abrams, 2012)

5/21/14 Full White - Pages 1-103 6/19/14 Rev Yellow - Pages 8-11, 24, 57, 58-62, 66, 68, 71, 82-82A, 84, 91-93, 97-100A, 103 6/20/14 Rev Green - Pages 22-22A, 27-27A, 64-65A, 68-68A, 90-91 555 Grant St., 5th Flr INT. GREG'S BEDROOM - NIGHT
A mediocre-looking teenage boy, GREG, is staring in frozen horror at a computer monitor, the only source of illumination in the room. He is lost in thought, and his thoughts are hell.
I have no idea how to tell this story.
He types. His typing is labored.
I don't even know how to start it. Like: I guess I could use one of those classic story-beginning sentences.
He examines the screen. There's one line written: "It was the best of times; it was the worst of times."
(becoming agitated) But what would that even mean? I mean, obviously somewhere in the world it's the best of times for someone.
INT. SOME GUY'S LIVING ROOM - DAY
Some EXTREMELY FORTUNATE GUY is benefitting from all these things that Greg is describing.

Like he's eating all of this insane Vietnamese food that he just got for free and the woman who delivered the food looks exactly like the hot girl from Pussy Riot and now she's situated in the corner playing unspeakably beautiful melodies on the harp. While he's just going to town on that food. So yeah. That's the best of times. Meanwhile,
INT. NORTH KOREAN DUNGEON - NIGHT - ALTHOUGH WHO REALLY KNOWS
A COMPARABLY UNFORTUNATE GUY is the recipient of Greg's imagined parade of horrors.

...some other guy is being tortured by the North Korean government specifically by being suspended over a crocodile-infested pool of acid, and because it's acid these crocodiles are just pissed, and they're also piping in that gross smell you get when they spill a bunch of milk in the school parking lot, and this beefy torturer dude is just punching the hell out of him. Worst of times. Check. BACK TO:
INT. GREG'S BEDROOM - NIGHT
GREG stares screenward. He has typed the words, "I have no idea what I am doing." He erases them and begins typing again.
All right. Look. I'll just start. This is the story of my senior year of high school, and how it destroyed my life, and how I made a film so bad it literally killed someone.

Greg finishes typing. The screen says: "I made a film so bad it literally killed someone." Greg is staring blankly at the screen, again. TITLE CARD: ME & EARL & THE DYING GIRL. The hot girl from Pussy Riot is back on harp.
INT. CITY BUS - 6:07 A.M., FIRST DAY OF SENIOR YEAR
GREG is slumped in a seat, yawning and absentmindedly eating something, as the bus rumbles through the darkness. SUPER: The part where I begin senior year.
I/E. SCHENLEY HIGH SCHOOL - BEFORE CLASS

Schenley High School is a stately, formidable city block of barred windows and desert-colored brick. It's a bit like they made a penitentiary out of a dismantled Gaza pyramid. As GREG walks through the school - down hallways, in and out of the band room, etc. - we trail him.
IN THE FOYER:
Greg approaches a lone JOCK.
I used to think about it this way: Like a continent, Schenley High School is divided into nations.
Greg and the jock exchange what's-up head nods.
Jock Nation.
IN A VAST GRITTY HALLWAY: Greg bumps fists with TWO STONERS, smoothly declining their offer to look at a YOUTUBE VIDEO.
Kingdom of Stoners.
FURTHER DOWN THE HALLWAY: JUSTIN HOWELL THE THEATER KID is laughing uproariously and flirtatiously at a thing Greg has said.
The People's Republic of Theater Dorks.
IN THE STAIRWELL:
Greg observes, unnoticed, as two STONERS pelt a JOCK with ketchup packets and then book it out of there.
In the typical high school life, you belong to one nation, which can never guarantee you total security.
IN THE HALLWAY: A group of GANGBANGERS smoking cigarettes exchanges sullen what's-up nods with Greg.
But I thought I found a way out. Get citizenship in EVERY nation. Get passports to EVERYWHERE.
IN THE BAND ROOM:
Greg briefly plays the bass drum in an impromptu jam session with FOUR OR FIVE BAND KIDS.
Just be on low-key good terms with everyone. Casually interact with them once in a while, in a way that is invisible to everyone else.
IN ANOTHER HALL:

Three MEDIOCRE-LOOKING GIRLS are in GREG'S path. One is quietly but utterly miserable. FRIEND OF MISERABLE GIRL The test was today? The miserable girl nods, wordless; her friends hug her fiercely, protectively.
Never commit to an interaction that won't be casual or mellow. That's like sending troops to Afghanistan.
(cheerily, hastily) Ugh! Tests! I've been there.
He speeds away-8
IN THE AUDITORIUM:
GREG is watching with polite interest as two GOTHY DORKS, including SCOTT MAYHEW, play Magic cards.
Maintain relationships even with citizens of the most dicked-upon nations. For example, Scott Mayhew, the Gothy dork I'm sitting next to here. It took years of cultivation to win his trust.
Scott plays a card entitled "Common Highland Berserker."
(murmuring respectfully) Scott, nice berserker.
Icy and sinister, Scott turns his gaze to Greg.
Thank you-9
PARKING LOT, STEPS:
GREG is patiently listening to the violent freestyle rapping of ILL PHIL, a runty neck-tattooed ne'er-do-well.
Or the universally ostracized Ill Phil. Truly, a nation of one.
They call me Ill Phil / I'll bend you to my will / my will is ill / and my name is Phil / for real /
Yeah, that's good.
(interrupting him) take you out back behind the mill / force you to eat a pill / now you like "what's the deal" / "I just got killed"
Some great rhymes in there.
This all may appear simple.
REWIND to the FRONT STEPS of school, where this scene began.
In fact, it requires thousands of social calculations per second.

REPLAY the interactions with the JOCK and the STONERS, again in slo-mo, but this time from GREG'S POV - ROBOGREG sequence. In his field of vision are SCI-FI VISUALS: bars and charts. It's the data with which a high-functioning autistic person might negotiate the terrifying social world of high school.
ROBO-GREG (V.O.)
DAJUAN WILLIAMS CONFIRMED INCOMING
execute low-key head nod CASUALLY TERMINATE HEAD NOD INCOMING: ALLAN MACCORMICK, JOSH
ALLAN MACCORMICK AND JOSH RAPAPORT
eye contact confirmed with allan
maintain speed; conceal sightlines 12 to 5 o'clock for fist bump
INT. CAFETERIA - LUNCHTIME
GREG, holding a bagged lunch, is standing inconspicuously at the entrance of the cafeteria. He is peering at A PLACE OF TOTAL CHAOS: crowded, dirty, and lawless.

And there were some places I simply couldn't go. Like the cafeteria. Every last square inch of it was disputed territory. It was Crimea, Kashmir, and the Gaza strip all rolled into one. Also the part of the Indian Ocean with pirates.
INT. MCCARTHY'S OFFICE

GREG is eating his lunch in a teacher's office, next to a DIMINUTIVE BLACK KID. Above them stands MR. MCCARTHY, a young teacher with a shaved head and arms sheathed in tattoos. He is notable for his paradoxically mellow intensity, and for his LOVE OF FACTS. He is, to Greg, the only reasonable teacher in all of
Instead, I always ate lunch in my history teacher's office...
FACT: I will be in 309 for the next 20 minutes. Respect the research.
(dutifully) Respect the research.
(chainsmoker's rasp) Respect the research.
McCarthy leaves. Greg hits space bar on McCarthy's DESKTOP to unpause a YOUTUBE CLIP (from BURDEN OF DREAMS: THE MAKING OF FITZCARRALDO). The boys watch and eat, wordlessly.

...with Earl, whose role in my life I'm not even going to try to explain to you right now. MADISON, probably the hottest girl in school and yet somehow also a fundamentally decent person, opens a door.
Oh hey guys.
Hi Madison.
Greg, how was your summer.
She smiles and touches Greg's arm. He suppresses a freak-out.

One last thing. Hot girls destroy your life. That's just a fact. It doesn't matter if the hot girl is also a good person. She's a moose, you're a chipmunk, she's just wandering through the forest, oblivious, and she doesn't even know that she stomped your head.
BRIEF DISPLAY OF IMAGE OF MOOSE STOMPING CHIPMUNK Back in Mr. McCarthy's office, Greg is attempting charm.
Summer. It's like... what does that word even mean? More "summ"? (beat) Winter: same deal! More "wint"?!
Mercifully, Earl cuts in.
McCarthy's in 309.
(brightly) Oh great! Thanks guys!
She leaves. Greg gazes wistfully at the closed door.
(still without looking up) Titties.
Greg continues to stare, lost in thought.
INT. GREG'S BEDROOM - AFTER SCHOOL
TITTIES are indeed what Greg is staring at, on his computer. A KNOCK ON THE DOOR sends him into panic. GREG
GREG'S MOM (entering) Honey? Can we come in?

GREG'S PARENTS are in the room now. GREG'S MOM is a forceful Jewish mom. She believes her son is the most wonderful person in the world, and also that she must take frequent intrusive measures to redirect his life. She is holding a shrinkwrapped COLLEGE DIRECTORY. Because this is a movie, she is probably sort of hot, but not egregiously so. GREG'S DAD, holding the family cat, is a muumuu-wearing classics professor, a man of profound spacey weirdness. He is often making a face of thoughtful concentration. This is to conceal the unfathomable strangeness within. Why is he thrusting the cat out at Greg? What is the cat's role in this impromptu family meeting? Unclear.
What do you want.
(tearing shrinkwrap) First, I was going through your stuff, and I saw you haven't even opened your college directory! So, please. Have a look.
Mom. Don't go through my stuff.
We discussed it, and she gets to go through your stuff.
Just have a look! It's fun! It's like a menu for your future! (paging through, theatrically) What are you in the mood for? Could I interest you in... Penn State? Pepperdine? Pomona? Ooh--Princeton?
I'm not getting into Princeton.
He's not getting into Princeton.
Well, not with that attitude. Victor, you're just being hostile because they wouldn't let you bring Cat Stevens into Whole Foods.
That establishment practices cat apartheid, and history will not judge them kindly for it.
Mom. Is that it?
It's not. Honey... your father and I wanted to talk to you about something kind of sad.
What? What happened?
I just got off the phone with Denise Kushner. Rachel's mom? You know Denise?
Not really.
You're friends with Rachel, though.
FLASHBACK - SCHENLEY SCHOOL HALLWAY:
The very sad mediocre-looking girl from earlier today? The one being comforted by her mediocre-looking friends? Yes. That was RACHEL.
INT. GREG'S BEDROOM - BACK TO THE PRESENT:
We're not friends, we're like... acquainted.
Honey, Rachel has been diagnosed with leukemia. They just found out.
FLASHBACK - SCHENLEY SCHOOL HALLWAY:
Hug of sadness.
Ugh! Tests! I've been there.
INT. GREG'S BEDROOM - BACK TO THE PRESENT:
(mortified at himself) Oh God. (beat) Is that serious?
(beginning to tear up) Oh honey. They don't know. They're doing tests, and they're gonna do all they can. But they just don't know.
(not sure what to say) That... sucks.
You're right. You're absolutely right. It does suck.
It sucks real bad.
I know. It sucks. It just really sucks, really really bad.
It sucks quite a bit. ALT: It sucks super hard.
She is crying. Hesitantly, Greg goes over to give her a hug. Greg's dad joins the hug. He is working the cat into the hug. They are all squat-hugging on the floor of Greg's bedroom.
Dad, Cat Stevens is biting me. GREG'S DAD He's deeply distraught. The hug stops. Cat Stevens scampers angrily away. GREG'S MOM Honey, Denise and I agreed, Rachel

Oh. Yeah. But, we're not really-ALT: Mom. You have to remember, we're not really-GREG'S MOM I know it's not easy. And it sucks. But that's why you should do it. And need to do it. It's a mitzvah. Just give Rachel a call.
(panicking) Mom. What do you want me to say? Hey, it's that random guy from school who's never really paid attention to you? But now you have cancer, so let's hang out?
That's not going to work. She'll think you're being sarcastic.

(beginning to lose patience) Honey? You really can't do this one nice thing for someone else? You're honestly telling me that you're-GREG UGGGGGGGGGGH FINE. (beat) But you have to stop going through my stuff! Or one of these days, I'm gonna go through your stuff.
I hope you like tampons.
INT. GREG'S HOUSE, TV ROOM - FIVE MINUTES LATER
GREG is on the phone, and his parents have left the room. The phone is ringing. INTERCUT WITH:
INT RACHEL'S HOUSE - DAY
RACHEL picks up.
Hi, this is Rachel.
Hey it's Greg Gaines.
Yo.
(unexcitedly)
(beat) Uh, I called a doctor. He said you needed a prescription of Greg-acil.
What's that.
Uh. It's me. Oh.
Uh, in convenient gel-tab form.
Oh. Yeahhhhh.
GREG This is excruciating.
So I guess you heard I'm sick. Yeahhhhh.
Did my mom tell you.
Uh, my mom told me. Oh. So, uh. (beat) What? What?
GREG
GREG
What were you going to say? Uhhh.
Greg, what?
I was calling... to see... if you wanted to hang out. Right now?
Uh... sure. No thanks.
Uh. You don't want to hang out?
No, thanks anyways.
Okay, uh... bye. Bye.
She hangs up. Greg feels like a colossal douchebag.
INT. GREG'S HOUSE, TV ROOM - SHORTLY AFTERWARDS
GREG is watching a movie, shamefaced. GREG'S MOM stomps into the room and switches off the TV.
Mom. She doesn't want to see me.
They size each other up.

I'm sorry to be the one to tell you this, Gregory, but you do not have a choice in this particular matter because you have been given the opportunity to make a very real difference in someone's life, and if what you're choosing to do instead is just lie around the house all day like a dead slug then I am required to step in and inform you that that is one

and if you think that any of these excuses you're giving me is more important than the happiness of a girl with cancer, a friend with cancer, then you have got another think coming, buddy, because you are going to pick up that phone, you are going to call Rachel, you are going to

(increasingly frantic) Mom. Can I just say something for one second? (beat) Mom. Just let me say something. Just for like one second. (beat) She doesn't want to hang out with me. We're not even friends. Mom! WE'RE NOT EVEN FRIENDS. (beat)
IS MAKING ME FREAK OUT AND
STATE. MOM. YOU HAVE MADE ME
EXT. RACHEL'S DOORSTEP - TEN MINUTES LATER
GREG is standing on the doorstep. He looks terrified.

Super: The part where I meet a dying girl The door opens. It's DENISE, Rachel's mom. Denise is a tough woman in impossible circumstances. She looks Greg up and down. He returns her gaze with illconcealed terror. Then suddenly she envelopes Greg in her wiry arms.
Gre-e-e-e-eg.
(slightly muffled) Hi Mrs. Kushner.
Denise, Greg. To you, I'm Denise.
(nervously) Okay! Good.
Denise leads him inside. On a table in an adjoining room is a bottle of something, and a glass. Dr. Phil is on.
You're a good kid. You know that? You're just a sweet, good-hearted kid. And handsome.
I'm not handsome, but thanks.
And so modest.
I'm a modest mouse, I guess.
HA. GREG. (teetering a little) Where do you come up with this stuff?
That's just a band name, I think-DENISE
RACHEL. THERE'S A MODEST LITTLE MOUSE HERE TO SEE YOU.
Rachel appears at the top of the steps. She is guarded. He is anxious. Denise winks at Greg and glides away.
Rachel-l-l-l.
Greg, what are you doing here.
Uh... So the doctor really recommends a strong dosage of Gregitor. He thinks you should start taking it immediately.
You already used that joke.

No, because last time it was about Greg-acil, which, if you recall, comes in convenient gel-tab form-RACHEL Look. I don't want you hanging out with me. I don't need your stupid pity. I'm fine. You can just go.
No no no. You've got it all wrong. I'm not here because I pity you. I'm here because my mom... (realizing that this is worse) ...is, uh... making me. Hmmmm.
That's actually worse.
(beginning to panic) I know. Look. Uh. I know.
Just leave, OK? Honestly. I'm fine.
(desperately) Rachel. Please listen to me. (he gathers himself) (MORE)
My mom is going to turn my life into a living hell if I don't hang out with you. She's basically the LeBron James of nagging. (beat) LeBron James plays--
I know who LeBron James is.
He realizes he has to beg.
Look. I understand that I'm not doing you a favor here. What I'm asking is for you to do me a favor.
You want a favor from me?
Yes. Please. Let me hang out with you for one day. I can tell my mom we hung out. Then we'll both be out of each other's lives. Deal?
Rachel considers this with narrowed eyes.
GREG
He holds out his fist for her to fist bump. But she is at the top of the stairs.
Is that a black-power salute?
No. I'm going for a fist bump.
I can't fist bump you from up here-GREG Yes. I realize that now.
INT. RACHEL'S BEDROOM - 30 SECONDS LATER
They stand near her bed in her girly room--the bed is covered in pillows, the walls are thick with magazine cutouts of actors. But it's also somewhat dark and cavelike, and there's not a lot of pink.
They sit down. Neither of them says anything. SUPER: EIGHT MINUTES LATER They are still on the bed, in slightly different positions.
So. Here we are. Hanging out with each other. Yeah.
Silence.
It's truly everything I ever hoped it would be.
(indicating random book on Rachel's desk) Uh... Sick book. Thanks.
(indicating bed) And, uh. Lot of pillows in here. How many pillows is that?
I don't know.
I wish I had that many pillows.
So ask your parents for some.
No, uh... they'd be suspicious or something.
That you'd sleep all the time?
They'd probably assume that I was planning to masturbate all over them.
A long beat. Rachel studies Greg. Greg studies Rachel. She might be charmed, and she might be existentially horrified. It's hard to tell.
They've got some really gross ideas about me. (beat) But that's on them. They need to stop getting sexy pillows.
She knows he wants her to laugh, and she is not going to give it to him yet. She maintains an admirable poker face. Greg reaches over and picks up a pillow.

This pillow is a dude, obviously. But he reminds me of a pillow we used to have. Francesca. Similar coloring. Oof, Francesca. In the end, we had to give her away. That whole situation was just... a problem. (poker face from Rachel) But there was also a chemistry between us that no one could deny. (still nothing [ALT: Rachel snorts]) The world thought it was wrong. But maybe the world was wrong. Wrong about what could be, between a pillow and a boy... who became a man.
Rachel snorts. ALT: Rachel bursts out laughing.
Or, I dunno.
No, that was good. Thank you.
But the moment is not allowed to develop - GREG'S PHONE
(checking phone) Oh damn. I'm sorry - I have to go.
It's okay. Who was that?
That was Earl.
Who's Earl?
Greg looks back at phone. ON PHONE: A selfie of EARL, menacing, and GREG'S DAD, dorkily content, both enjoying DRIED CUTTLEFISH. Plus the message: WHERE U AT?? DUMBASS IMMA EAT ALL UR SQUID
INT. MCCARTHY'S OFFICE - FIRST DAY OF SCHOOL - LUNCHTIME FB11
We're back to the first day of school, where GREG was eating lunch in a teacher's office with EARL.
You may remember Earl from 15 minutes ago. Titties.
He continues to eat his lunch, looking pissed.
So, some people think Earl is my friend. But he's really not. He's more like a coworker. I've known him since kindergarten.
EXT. IN FRONT OF EARL'S HOUSE - ONE DAY MANY YEARS AGO

Earl lives in a ramshackle house with gutters falling off. His much-tattooed brother DERRICK slouches and smokes on the porch. Derrick watches with menace as YOUNG GREG, wearing a backpack, cautiously approaches from the sidewalk.
His house is a short walk from mine, but in a much tougher neighborhood. His dad's in Texas, his mom's a depressed shut-in, and his brother Derrick's dog, Doopie, will definitely eat me someday.
Derrick chuckles, snaps his fingers, and a GIANT DOG explodes through the front door, barking furiously. The dog chases Greg off-camera. YOUNG EARL comes running out after them.
Doopie! Doopie, chill.
EXT. IN FRONT OF GREG'S HOUSE - MANY YEARS AGO
Similar shot, except of a much nicer house in a leafy neighborhood. YOUNG GREG AND YOUNG EARL stroll up the walk, past Cat Stevens, who is asleep.
So over the years, we've mostly hung out at my place.
What you got, cat. You wanna fight? Didn't think so. Punk-ass cat.
INT. GREG'S TV ROOM - MANY YEARS AGO
YOUNG GREG AND YOUNG EARL happen upon GREG'S DAD, who is watching Aguirre, the Wrath of God.
My house has better stuff to do anyway.
Boys, you'll want to pay close attention to this. The insane conquistador Aguirre is raging through the jungle, in search of a golden city that does not exist.
The boys are transfixed. On-screen, Aguirre is freaking out.
It's a classic of foreign cinema.
(happily) Dude's got issues.
INT. GREG'S KITCHEN - MANY YEARS AGO
GREG'S DAD is serving the BOYS cuttlefish.
In addition to the best films, my house also has the weirdest food.
This is cuttlefish, a sea creature much like a squid. It is a favorite East Asian snack food.
(chewing) Staaaaaaank.
Yes, the smell is odd and repellent to our Western noses.
Obviously we come from pretty different backgrounds. But somehow we like most of the same things.
INT. GREG'S TV ROOM - MANY YEARS AGO

YOUNG GREG AND YOUNG EARL are eating more cuttlefish and trying to watch The Seventh Seal. Next to them, however, three of their CLASSMATES are munching Doritos and watching a fourth classmate play PS3.
And we learned pretty early on that we were the only ones who liked, for example, classics of foreign cinema.
EXT. A PARK NEAR GREG'S HOUSE - MANY YEARS AGO
YOUNG GREG AND YOUNG EARL are trying to recreate a scene from Rashomon. Young Greg has a camera and a boom mike. Young Earl, dressed like a samurai, is sort of spazzing out.

Why did we like them? It's hard to say. Maybe it's that they were weird and often violent, like us. Or confusing and possibly meaningless, like life. Anyway, we liked them so much that we started making our own.
INT. GREG'S LIVING ROOM - MANY YEARS AGO
Now we are watching one of their films. YOUNG GREG AND YOUNG EARL, dressed like samurai, are murdering each other and knocking over living room furniture.
We've made 42 films.

CUE QUICK CUTS of the GAINES/JACKSON OEUVRE, intercut with shots of them making their films: IN THE GAINES BACKYARD: GREG'S MOM picks herbs from the garden - a CAMERA-EQUIPPED TOY HELICOPTER lowers jerkily into her hair, startling her nearby, YOUNG GREG sheepishly holds the controller while YOUNG EARL tries to set him straight -
Down is up! DOWN is UP. How you still not getting this.
You keep saying that but it makes no sense!
ON THE GAINES PORCH:
GREG'S DAD, attempting to grill SWEETBREADS, looks down in irritation - he has stepped onto some tracks and a CAMERAEQUIPPED TONKA TRUCK is banging into his feet -
Boys! These sweetbreads need my absolute focus!
Just try not to walk on the tracks!

ALT: The Tonka truck has cornered CAT STEVENS, who is swatting at it irately while wearing ill-fitting clothes - ALT: GREG'S DAD sets the dining room table, oblivious to the MELODRAMATIC SCREAMING coming from outside and ABUNDANCE OF FAKE BLOOD repeatedly spattering against the window -
They're all pretty horrible, but for some reason we keep making them. They all have the same signature ending.
Young Earl has murdered Young Greg. He yells something in a made-up language at the camera.
Life is the shadow of death. BLACKOUT, plus the giant words, "NOW YOU ARE DEAD."
INT. GREG'S LIVING ROOM - PRESENT
GREG and EARL have just finished re-watching their movie. They are both munching squid. GREG'S DAD
Dad! For like the billionth time! You're not allowed to watch these!
Greg's dad shuffles away.
So. You gonna see that girl again?
(nonchalant) I mean... probably. Yeah.
Earl processes this. A sweet little beat.
You gonna eat her pussy? ALT: gonna play with them titties?
NO. Come on. It's not like that.
Well, that ain't right. Might be her last chance on earth to be with a man. Can't make this about you.
Earl. First of all, if it's that high-stakes, I probably won't even be able to get a boner.
Did I say shit about boners? No. (beat) Now what kinda cancer even is acute myelogenous leukemia.
You know. Cancer of the... thing.
EXT./INT. MCCARTHY'S CLASSROOM - THE NEXT DAY
Super: The part where I am even more of an idiot than normal The bell has just rung on the history class of MR. MCCARTHY.
Okay. Class. Tomorrow, you must come armed with an epic fact. Just one fact. But it must be epic. CLASS. RESPECT THE RESEARCH.
(half-heartedly) Respect the research. Mr. McCarthy punches his own biceps. FACTS.
Greg approaches him as his classmates file out of the room.
Mr. McCarthy? Do you know any facts about, uh, leukemia?
(kindly) Yes. Leukemia is cancer of the blood and/or bone marrow.
So it's pretty spread out in the body.
Own the fact. Yes.
How soon do people die from it?
Well, it's often very treatable, bud. Why do you ask?
You know Rachel Kushner has leukemia, right? This silences MR. MCCARTHY.
Also OTHER NEARBY CLASSMATES (including MADISON). In retrospect, Greg has said this way too loud.
Rachel has what?
INT. RACHEL'S BEDROOM - THAT EVENING
GREG is back in RACHEL'S pillow-infested room. She is not happy. He has his head in his hands.
I'm, like, innovatively stupid.
Everyone was going to find out sooner or later. I just hate having to share everything about myself.
Greg is trying to think of a way to help.
I'm the exact same way. Check it out. One thing you can do when you don't want to deal with people is just enter a subhuman state.
Rachel looks at him expressionlessly.
Here, pretend you're someone annoying.
(impersonating an annoying classmate) Hi, Rachel. I'm really sorry you have cancer.
(cross-eyed, sort of zombie-like) urrrrjj jjjunhjh uuhjjghjnj
Rachel does not know what to think of this. It's definitely cute. It's also deeply stupid.
Does that ever work?
It works all the time. It's passive resistance. That's what Gandhi was all about.
I'm pretty sure Gandhi never did the subhuman thing.
How do you think India achieved statehood? Try it: uurrrnng nnnurrrrjjrjjjj.
(smiling) Nope.
Come on! It's easy. Or another thing you can do is just flat-out pretend to be dead. Check it out. Say something annoying to me.
(impersonating another annoying classmate) Hi, Rachel. I just wanted you to remember that your cancer is all part of God's plan.
Greg is being flamboyantly dead. Rachel is enjoying this. But behind her head, a cut-out of HUGH JACKMAN glares at Greg.
Yo. Asshole. Just so we're straight on this: You're advising a girl with cancer to pretend to be dead?
(nervously trying to ignore Hugh Jackman) urrngh
No, seriously. Think about what you're doing here, dickhead. Jesus.
Greg is now lying there with a horrified look on his face.

I've been doing my broody Wolverine face on this girl's wall for five and a quarter years, and at this point I'm probably only still here because she'd feel weirdly guilty or disloyal taking me down, but I'm goddamned if I'm letting a little punk like you waltz in here stupiding up the place-RACHEL Greg, what's wrong?
Uh... (foolishly) Sorry, I shouldn't have told you to pretend to be dead. It was really insensitive.
I mean... I'm sick. I'm not dying.
(panicking) No yeah obviously but now I'm being all weird about it, and I can't get un-weird, because I'm just an idiot and despite what you just said I'm clearly still sitting here thinking
